segments.title: HMS Rutherford’s American-built origin
segments.summary: Meet HMS Rutherford, a Captain-class frigate built in the United States as a Buckley-class destroyer escort and employed by the Royal Navy from 1943 to 1945.
segments.body: Rutherford was K558, an American-built escort that entered Royal Navy service as a Captain-class frigate. She had originally been constructed in the United States as a Buckley-class destroyer escort, so the two classifications describe the same hull in American and British service. Her Royal Navy career ran from 1943 to 1945, placing her coastal-forces work in the last years of the Second World War. This opening is useful for understanding how a U.S. escort design became a British wartime command ship.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies K558 as a Captain-class frigate built as a Buckley-class destroyer escort and gives her Royal Navy service dates.

segments.title: An escort designed for reach rather than speed
segments.summary: Rutherford’s dimensions, boiler plant, 24-knot top speed, and 5,500-nautical-mile range show why an escort’s endurance could matter more than high speed.
segments.body: The video gives Rutherford a displacement of roughly 1,400 tons and a compact hull about 93 metres long, 11 metres wide, and 2.7 metres in draught. Two Foster Wheeler express D-type water-tube boilers produced about 13,500 shaft horsepower. That plant gave her a maximum speed of roughly 24 knots, which Dr. Clarke stresses was sufficient rather than exceptional. Her more revealing performance figure was a range of about 5,500 nautical miles at 15 knots. The comparison shows why an escort's ability to cover long routes and remain useful on station could matter more than high pursuit speed.

evidence.note: The transcript lists the frigate’s displacement, dimensions, boiler plant, speed, and endurance.

segments.title: Anti-aircraft and anti-submarine weapons for coastal work
segments.summary: Rutherford combined three-inch and Bofors guns with a growing Oerlikon battery, Hedgehog, and depth charges for the changing threats of the Channel war.
segments.body: Rutherford carried three-inch guns and a twin 40 mm Bofors mount as her heavier gun armament. Her battery of 20 mm Oerlikons varied from seven to sixteen weapons, with more fitted as D-Day approached. A Mark 10 Hedgehog anti-submarine mortar and depth charges gave the frigate a separate means of attacking submerged threats. The changing close-range battery illustrates how wartime escorts were adapted as the air threat intensified. Taken together, the weapons show a coastal-forces command ship equipped to defend itself against both aircraft and submarines.

evidence.note: The transcript lists the gun armament, the enlarged 20 mm battery near D-Day, Hedgehog, and depth charges.

segments.title: A radar-equipped command ship for motor torpedo boats
segments.summary: Radar, sonar, and direction-finding equipment made Rutherford an information hub that could direct smaller motor torpedo boats during coastal operations.
segments.body: Rutherford's SA and SL radar sets, Type 144 sonar, and medium- and high-frequency direction-finding antennas mattered as much as her guns. Dr. Clarke describes a command ship as a sensor node that gathered information and distributed it to smaller craft. In Rutherford's case, those recipients included motor torpedo boats whose size limited the equipment they could carry themselves. The Royal Navy therefore used coastal-forces command frigates to give a flotilla a stronger information and coordination centre. A destroyer or destroyer flotilla leader could sometimes fill the same role because it carried comparable sensors and additional capability. This passage makes Rutherford useful as a compact example of command-and-control being built around information collection rather than firepower alone.

evidence.note: The transcript lists Rutherford’s sensors and explains her information-sharing command role alongside motor torpedo boats and occasional destroyer flotilla leaders.
